claims.claim_text: The opening healthcare SaaS audit story with January 2024 timing, $4.2M, 14 EKS clusters, 6 vulnerable clusters, 90 days, and the CISO quote is presented as a real event.
claims.span_hint: line 21 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: In January 2024, a healthcare SaaS company passed their SOC 2 Type II audit with flying colors. The financial impact of this certification was immense; it unblocked $4.2 million in enterprise contracts that had been stalled in procurement. Three months later, an automated cloud security scanner deployed by a newly hired platform engineer discovered that 14 of their production EKS clusters had Kubernetes audit logging completely disabled. Furthermore, 6 clusters were running backend containers with known critical CVEs, and their primary database namespace had no network policies enforcing ingress restrictions. None of these issues existed during the audit snapshot. They had accumulated silently in the 90 days since the auditor signed off. The company's CISO described the situation as "compliance theater" — they were compliant on audit day, but wildly non-compliant every other day of the year.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A team can pass an audit and still drift materially out of compliance soon afterward if logging, vulnerability management, and network controls are not continuously monitored and enforced.
claims.rationale: This is a precise unattributed anecdote about a real company and event, so it cannot be kept without a verifiable source.

claims.claim_text: A compliance sprint typically lasts 6 weeks before an audit.
claims.span_hint: line 50 | section: The Traditional Compliance Model (Broken)
claims.anchor_text: **"Compliance Sprint"**: 6 weeks of panic before an audit.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **"Compliance Sprint"**: a period of intense manual preparation before an audit.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited precise duration used as fact.

claims.claim_text: Organizations are compliant for roughly 2 months per year and non-compliant for roughly 10 months.
claims.span_hint: line 51 | section: The Traditional Compliance Model (Broken)
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Actual Status**: often compliant only around the audit window, with drift accumulating during the rest of the year.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited specific annual breakdown.

claims.claim_text: A mature continuous-compliance program will have 12 months of immutable evidence for every control.
claims.span_hint: line 69 | section: The Continuous Compliance Model (What You Want)
claims.anchor_text: "Here is the dashboard. Every control has 12 months of continuous, immutable evidence."
claims.suggested_rewrite: "Here is the dashboard. Every control has a continuous, immutable evidence trail."
claims.rationale: The exact 12-month evidence window is presented as fact without support.

claims.claim_text: An evidence pipeline will already have been collecting proof every six hours, cryptographically signing it, and storing it in a WORM-compliant bucket.
claims.span_hint: line 71 | section: The Continuous Compliance Model (What You Want)
claims.anchor_text: The automated pipeline has already been doing that every six hours, cryptographically signing the output, and storing it in a WORM (Write Once, Read Many) compliant storage bucket.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A mature evidence pipeline can collect this data on a fixed schedule, sign the artifacts, and store them in immutable WORM-capable storage.
claims.rationale: The cadence and implementation details are too specific to leave uncited.

claims.claim_text: AWS Security Hub aggregates findings from 50+ AWS services.
claims.span_hint: line 83 | section: Cloud-Native CSPM Tools table
claims.suggested_rewrite: Aggregates findings from multiple AWS services and supported integrations
claims.rationale: The numeric service count is marketing-sensitive and not stably backed by a single authoritative page.

claims.claim_text: Google Security Command Center exposure for GKE includes the Security Posture Dashboard and workload vulnerability scanning capabilities.
claims.span_hint: line 85 | section: Cloud-Native CSPM Tools table
claims.rationale: The GKE security posture dashboard docs explicitly describe posture scanning and workload vulnerability scanning.

claims.claim_text: Prisma Cloud is the most comprehensive option and also the most expensive.
claims.span_hint: line 86 | section: Cloud-Native CSPM Tools table
claims.rationale: This is an unsupported superlative plus a pricing judgment.

claims.claim_text: Wiz provides agentless Kubernetes scanning and graph-based risk analysis without agents.
claims.span_hint: line 87 | section: Cloud-Native CSPM Tools table
claims.rationale: This appears real and vendor-documented, but the primary source is on an off-allowlist domain.

claims.claim_text: Aqua Security has the strongest open-source ecosystem, including Trivy and Tracee.
claims.span_hint: line 88 | section: Cloud-Native CSPM Tools table
claims.suggested_rewrite: Notable open-source ties including Trivy and Tracee
claims.rationale: The superlative is not objectively supportable.

claims.claim_text: Amazon Inspector automatically scans ECR images and reports CVEs to Security Hub.
claims.span_hint: line 113 | section: AWS Security Hub + EKS Integration
claims.rationale: Amazon Inspector documentation covers continuous ECR scanning and Security Hub integration.

claims.claim_text: Microsoft Defender for Cloud provides CSPM capabilities for AKS and assesses clusters against security baselines with remediation guidance.
claims.span_hint: lines 128-129 | section: Microsoft Defender for Cloud + AKS
claims.rationale: Microsoft Learn describes Defender CSPM hardening guidance, secure score, and ongoing assessment.

claims.claim_text: SOC 2 focuses on security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy.
claims.span_hint: line 166 | section: SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria
claims.rationale: The AICPA Trust Services Criteria are the right primary source, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Trivy Operator automatically rescans active workloads whenever the vulnerability database updates, usually daily.
claims.span_hint: line 435 | section: Trivy Operator for In-Cluster Scanning
claims.anchor_text: By deploying the Trivy Operator inside your cluster, you enable continuous evaluation of running workloads. Whenever the vulnerability database is updated (usually daily), Trivy automatically rescans the active workloads and surfaces newly published CVEs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By deploying the Trivy Operator inside your cluster, you enable continuous evaluation of running workloads. It automatically generates vulnerability reports for cluster workloads and updates them as workloads change.
claims.rationale: The exact update trigger and timing are not cleanly supported by the upstream material reviewed.

claims.claim_text: Falco's eBPF probes monitor system calls in real time.
claims.span_hint: line 539 | section: Falco for Runtime Compliance
claims.anchor_text: Falco's eBPF probes monitor these system calls in real-time.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Falco monitors runtime events and can alert on suspicious behavior in real time.
claims.rationale: The reviewed upstream sources support Falco runtime monitoring, but this sentence is too specific about the implementation path.

claims.claim_text: The CIS Kubernetes Benchmark provides prescriptive guidance for secure Kubernetes configuration.
claims.span_hint: line 596 | section: CIS Benchmark Scanning and Automated Remediation
claims.rationale: The benchmark is real and authoritative, but the primary CIS domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: SOC 2 audits cost $30,000-$200,000, continuous compliance cuts prep cost by 60-70%, compliance sprints take 400-600 engineering hours, and continuous compliance reduces this to under 40 hours.
claims.span_hint: line 636 | section: Did You Know? #1
claims.anchor_text: SOC 2 audits cost between $30,000 and $200,000 per engagement, depending on scope and auditor. Companies that implement continuous compliance tooling report spending 60-70% less on audit preparation because evidence is pre-collected and always current. The average "compliance sprint" before a traditional audit consumes 400-600 engineering hours. Continuous compliance reduces this to under 40 hours of auditor interaction time.
claims.suggested_rewrite: SOC 2 audits can be expensive and time-consuming, and teams with mature continuous-compliance tooling often spend much less effort gathering evidence during the audit itself.
claims.rationale: This is a bundle of precise business-impact numbers with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: PCI-DSS v4.0 was released in March 2022 and introduced a customized approach alongside the defined approach.
claims.span_hint: line 637 | section: Did You Know? #2
claims.rationale: PCI SSC is the right primary source, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A service mesh providing mTLS can be argued to satisfy PCI requirement 4.2.1 even though the original requirement was written for TLS on web servers.
claims.span_hint: line 637 | section: Did You Know? #2
claims.anchor_text: For Kubernetes environments, this is significant: you can argue that a service mesh providing mTLS meets PCI requirement 4.2.1 (strong cryptography for transmitting cardholder data) even though the original requirement was written for TLS on web servers.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For Kubernetes environments, this is significant: a customized approach can let you document alternative technical controls that satisfy a requirement's objective, subject to assessor review.
claims.rationale: This is a specific interpretive compliance conclusion that should not be presented as settled fact without authoritative backing.

claims.claim_text: Trivy detects vulnerabilities in OS packages, language-specific dependencies, IaC misconfigurations, and Kubernetes manifests.
claims.span_hint: line 638 | section: Did You Know? #3
claims.rationale: The Trivy upstream README explicitly lists supported targets and scanners covering these areas.

claims.claim_text: Trivy scans over 1 billion container images per month across its user base.
claims.span_hint: line 638 | section: Did You Know? #3
claims.anchor_text: Trivy, the open-source vulnerability scanner, scans over 1 billion container images per month across its user base.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Trivy is a widely used open-source vulnerability scanner.
claims.rationale: This is a large usage statistic with no authoritative cited source in the module.

claims.claim_text: A single Trivy scan of an average container image takes 8-15 seconds and checks against more than 150,000 known CVEs.
claims.span_hint: line 638 | section: Did You Know? #3
claims.anchor_text: A single Trivy scan of an average container image takes 8-15 seconds and checks against a database of over 150,000 known CVEs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Trivy scans are fast enough to fit into routine CI and cluster workflows, and its coverage depends on the vulnerability data sources it uses.
claims.rationale: Both the timing and database-size figures are precise and unsupported here.

claims.claim_text: Average time from CVE publication to exploit availability fell from 45 days in 2020 to 15 days in 2024.
claims.span_hint: line 639 | section: Did You Know? #4
claims.anchor_text: The average time from CVE publication to exploit availability decreased from 45 days in 2020 to 15 days in 2024, according to Mandiant's threat intelligence reports.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Exploit timelines can be short and continue to shrink, which makes faster detection and remediation more important.
claims.rationale: This is a precise time-series statistic without a directly cited primary source.

claims.claim_text: Quarterly vulnerability scans almost certainly leave exploitable containers in production, while Trivy Operator catches new CVEs within hours of database updates and cuts exposure from months to hours.
claims.span_hint: line 639 | section: Did You Know? #4
claims.anchor_text: This means organizations running quarterly vulnerability scans are almost certainly running exploitable containers. Continuous scanning with tools like Trivy Operator catches new CVEs within hours of database updates, reducing the exposure window from months to hours.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Infrequent vulnerability scans leave long blind spots; more frequent or continuous scanning reduces how long newly disclosed issues can go unnoticed.
claims.rationale: The certainty and timing claims are too strong and too specific for the support available.

claims.claim_text: Manual evidence collection does not scale past 3 clusters.
claims.span_hint: line 646 | section: Common Mistakes table
claims.anchor_text: Manual collection does not scale past 3 clusters.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Manual collection becomes difficult to sustain as cluster count grows.
claims.rationale: The three-cluster threshold is a precise unsupported limit.

claims.claim_text: Recommended remediation SLAs are Critical = 24 hours, High = 7 days, Medium = 30 days.
claims.span_hint: line 650 | section: Common Mistakes table
claims.anchor_text: Define SLAs: Critical = 24 hours, High = 7 days, Medium = 30 days.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Define and document vulnerability-remediation SLAs based on your risk tolerance and regulatory obligations.
claims.rationale: These are specific prescriptive deadlines without a cited governing source.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Auditing
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream reference for audit policy levels, audit logging behavior, and evidence-collection mechanics.

further_reading.title: GuardDuty EKS Protection
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for the module's EKS-focused discussion of audit-log monitoring and threat findings.

further_reading.title: GKE Security Posture Dashboard
further_reading.why_relevant: Directly covers GKE posture scanning and workload vulnerability features referenced in the CSPM section.

further_reading.title: kube-bench
further_reading.why_relevant: Practical upstream tool reference for running CIS-oriented Kubernetes hardening checks.

notes: No section pool exists for this section, so section_pool_ref is null and source_ids remain empty. Mermaid diagrams, code blocks, hands-on solution blocks, and quiz items were excluded per instructions; I only seeded table cells where they contained vendor/statistic claims or unsupported precise factual claims.
